intro:
Profit margin is the lifeblood of any business — it tells you what percentage of revenue you actually keep after covering costs. Whether you are pricing a product, evaluating a business, or deciding whether to expand, profit margin is the fundamental metric that separates sustainable businesses from ones that are simply busy.

Our profit margin calculator instantly computes gross profit, gross margin percentage, and markup percentage from your revenue and cost inputs. Understanding the difference between margin and markup is crucial: a 25% markup is not the same as 25% margin, and confusing them can lead to serious pricing errors.

For retailers, typically target 20–50% gross margins. Software companies often exceed 70%. Grocery stores may operate on 1–3% net margins. Benchmarking your margins against industry averages is essential context for evaluating business performance.
howItWorks: Enter your selling price (revenue per unit) and total cost per unit. The calculator computes gross profit (revenue minus cost), gross margin percentage (profit as a percentage of revenue), and markup percentage (profit as a percentage of cost). All three metrics are displayed simultaneously for complete pricing clarity.
interpretationGuide: Gross margin shows what percentage of each sale is profit before operating expenses. Markup shows how much you added to your cost to reach the selling price. A 50% gross margin means you keep $0.50 of every $1 in revenue. A 100% markup means you doubled the cost to set the price.

faqs.question: What is the difference between profit margin and markup?
faqs.answer: Profit margin is profit divided by revenue (selling price). Markup is profit divided by cost. A 50% markup on a $10 cost gives a $15 selling price — but the margin is 33.3% ($5 profit ÷ $15 revenue). Always clarify which metric is being referenced to avoid pricing errors.

faqs.question: What is gross profit vs. net profit?
faqs.answer: Gross profit is revenue minus the direct cost of goods sold (COGS). Net profit deducts all expenses including overhead, salaries, marketing, rent, taxes, and interest. This calculator computes gross profit margin — to get net margin, subtract all other operating expenses.

faqs.question: What is a good profit margin?
faqs.answer: It varies dramatically by industry. Software/SaaS companies often achieve 60–80% gross margins. Retail is typically 20–50%. Restaurants average 3–9% net margins. Services businesses like consulting can reach 30–50% net margins. Always compare to your specific industry benchmark.

faqs.question: How do I use margin to set prices?
faqs.answer: If you know your cost and target margin, use: Selling Price = Cost ÷ (1 − Target Margin). For a $20 cost with a 40% target margin: $20 ÷ 0.60 = $33.33. Many business owners mistakenly calculate $20 × 1.40 = $28, which only yields 28.6% margin, not 40%.

faqs.question: What is break-even analysis?
faqs.answer: Break-even is the point where total revenue equals total costs — zero profit and zero loss. Break-Even Units = Fixed Costs ÷ (Selling Price − Variable Cost Per Unit). Knowing your break-even point helps you understand the minimum volume needed to cover all expenses.

faqs.question: How does volume affect profit margin?
faqs.answer: Higher volume typically improves margins through economies of scale: fixed costs spread across more units, volume purchasing discounts lower COGS, and manufacturing efficiency increases. This is why growth often dramatically improves profitability even without price increases.

faqs.question: What is contribution margin?
faqs.answer: Contribution margin is selling price minus variable costs only (not fixed costs). It shows how much each unit sold contributes toward covering fixed costs and generating profit. Contribution margin = Selling Price − Variable Costs per unit.

faqs.question: Should I lower prices to increase volume?
faqs.answer: It depends on your margin and volume elasticity. If you cut price by 10% with a 20% margin, you need to increase volume by 100% just to maintain the same total profit. Low-margin businesses are particularly vulnerable to price cuts — use this calculator to model the impact before discounting.

faqs.question: What is EBITDA margin?
faqs.answer: EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) margin is a common metric for evaluating operational profitability. It excludes non-cash charges and financing effects, making it useful for comparing operational efficiency across companies. EBITDA ÷ Revenue = EBITDA Margin.

faqs.question: How do I improve my profit margin?
faqs.answer: Increase revenue (raise prices, sell more volume, add higher-margin products), reduce COGS (negotiate supplier contracts, improve production efficiency), or reduce operating expenses (automate processes, reduce overhead). Most businesses find the greatest leverage in reducing COGS through better purchasing or production.
